Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adjective + neuter noun formula; nominal idiom often used as object or predicate complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A grave wrong, serious offense, or morally heavy transgression.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: idālu ḫandātar iyawar — doing a bad act.
Usage Contexts: law, ritual confession, royal admonition, moral judgment
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Grounded in Hittite legal and ritual language where wrongdoing is framed as a burden before gods and community.
Second Example Sentence: ANA DINGIR.MEŠ šalliš waštul UL tarnanzi.
Third Example Sentence: mān šalliš waštul ḫarzi, nu=kan arḫa šipandanzi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adjective + accusative noun + finite verb; verbal idiom with direct object.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To speak maliciously, slander, or deliver harmful speech.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: idālawar tē- — to say something bad.
Usage Contexts: court speech, family conflict, diplomacy, accusations
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects the high value of oath-bound and ritually controlled speech in Hittite public life.
First Example Sentence: nu=mu kuit idālu memiyan mematti, nu=mu karū naḫšariyami.
Second Example Sentence: LÚ.MEŠ URU-Ḫattuša idālu memiyan UL išdamaššanunun.
Third Example Sentence: mān apāš idālu memiyan memai, nu LUGAL-uš ḫandātar šākzi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adjective + accusative noun + light verb; verbal-nominal idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To give favorable counsel, make peaceable speech, or say something beneficial.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: aššul tē- — to say good things.
Usage Contexts: diplomacy, mediation, family advice, temple instruction
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connected to Hittite diplomatic and ritual ideals of ordered, beneficial speech.
First Example Sentence: nu DUMU-aš attaš aššu memiyan iyazi.
Third Example Sentence: kuitman uddar ḫandāit, nu aššu memiyan iyandu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Ablative noun phrase + directional particle + transitive verb; metaphorical release construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To let resentment go, forgive, or release a matter emotionally.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: waštul UL appa mema- — not mention the offense again.
Usage Contexts: reconciliation, prayer, family dispute, royal mercy
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses kardiya- ‘heart’ as the seat of intention and emotion, a recurrent ancient Anatolian metaphor.
First Example Sentence: nu=za apēl waštul kardiyaz arḫa tarnahhun.
Second Example Sentence: mān annaš karū nakkiyawan ḫarzi, nu kardiyaz arḫa tarnau.
Third Example Sentence: LUGAL-uš kuit karpiya, nu=za uddar kardiyaz arḫa tarnai.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Accusative abstract noun + transitive verb; light-verb idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To put one’s mind or soul toward something; to resolve, intend, or commit inwardly.
Usage Contexts: prayer, royal decision, personal resolve, ritual vow
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Built on ištanza- ‘soul, mind, will’, an important concept in Hittite ritual and religious discourse.
Second Example Sentence: mān tuel ištanzan daitti, nu uttar ḫandāi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Plural/body-part noun + local particle + intransitive verb; perception metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To become visible, evident, or undeniable.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on the eyes as the site of recognition and proof, common in ancient evidentiary phrasing.
Second Example Sentence: mān tuppianza išḫi, nu ḫandātar šakuwaš anda uizzi.
Third Example Sentence: idālu memiyan šakuwaš anda uit, nu LÚ.MEŠ išdamaššer.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Accusative legal noun + transitive verb; institutional light-verb construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To impose, establish, or enter into a binding obligation or treaty.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: linkiyan iya- — to make an oath.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in Hittite treaty culture, where obligations were ritually witnessed and politically binding.
Second Example Sentence: mān išḫiul dātten, nu linkiyaš DINGIR.MEŠ išdamaššanzi.
Third Example Sentence: URU-aš LÚ.MEŠ išḫiul dāer nu aššul ḫarkanzi.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Sumerographic object + transitive verb; royal-symbolic idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To hold authority, rule, or possess legitimate command.
Usage Contexts: royal inscriptions, politics, succession, authority claims
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: The staff or sceptre functions as a symbol of royal and administrative power in ancient Near Eastern court language.
First Example Sentence: LUGAL-uš GIŠGIDRU ḫarzi nu KUR-e ḫandāizzi.
Second Example Sentence: mān DUMU.LUGAL GIŠGIDRU ḫarkzi, nu LÚ.MEŠ ašanzi.
Third Example Sentence: GIŠGIDRU kuit ḫarkanzi, nu uddar=šet nakki.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Genitive plural noun + postposition + transitive/existential verb; ritual accountability idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To hold a matter before the gods; to place it under divine witness or judgment.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: DINGIR.MEŠ-ni uttar mema- — to tell the matter to the gods.
Usage Contexts: ritual, oath, confession, curse formula, royal prayer
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Hittite statecraft and ritual practice frequently cast legal and political obligations before divine witnesses.
Second Example Sentence: mān linkiyaš arḫa pai, nu DINGIR.MEŠ-aš peran ḫarkanzi.
Third Example Sentence: waštul=ma DINGIR.MEŠ-aš peran ḫarta, nu LUGAL-uš naḫšariyat.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Accusative abstract noun + transitive verb; idiomatic possession/state construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To maintain peace, well-being, or favorable order.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: aššu ēš- — to be well or peaceful.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on aššu-/aššul ‘good, well-being’, central to Hittite formulaic wishes and orderly social relations.
Second Example Sentence: KUR-e aššul ḫarkanzi nu LÚ.MEŠ šer ašanzi.
